speaker: interviewer
text: So how did it come about that you came to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, it was very cold. We came in July to Bonegilla Camp, which is near Albury. And they were army barracks, but they were tin army barracks. It was so-- I thought Australia, sunny Australia, you know. And it-- it was just so awful. I thought I'm going to die in that. Not-heated, and we had some of those army horrible blankets, hard blankets. So I thought, no, that's no good. So I went to the chief of the hospital. And I spoke a little bit English. Luckily one year of my poor education I spent in an all-English school. So I had some knowledge, not very much. And I said to him-- I had a certificate from Bagnoli, from Italy, that I was an x-ray assistant. And I showed it to him. And I said, I would like to work in the hospital. And he says, oh lovely, lovely. You know, they didn't have [LAUGHS] anybody who spoke English. So I did all the x-rays of the migrants that came in and had to have chest-- chest x-rays in-- in the camp. And then I went to Sydney. I-- I was still in the camp there. And they sent us wherever they wanted. It was a two-year contract that you had to go where they sent you to. But I had some relatives in-- in-- in Sydney. And they took me to a Mr. [? Tieman, ?] who had [? Austie ?]. It was a great big factory. And because I was doing textile after the war, the woven things, I-- I talked him into it, to apply for me as a specialist in textiles. And I went back to Bonegilla. That was a time, it was called Spirit of Progress that took 16 hours from here to the border of New South Wales.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I was back in Bonegilla, you know, doing my job and waiting. And really he did it. And-- and then I got my two years in his factory, at [? Austie, ?] which was fantastic. And that was-- that was a good start. And I think it was very important that they did the migration like they did it. We all had our jobs. And we had to do the two years. And after the two years, you could do what you wanted, and you were free to go. And we were not obliging on-- on the taxpayers. And I think it was a much better system of migration than we have today. How well do you think these events that you went through, how do you think they have affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Well, I think it would be a better [INAUDIBLE]. I think most people think I'm crazy. But I think that I'm terribly disciplined. I have a lot of courage. And I'm very compassionate. But I don't like the human race at all. And I don't think it came from the camps. When I came back after the war, I had no hatred for the human race. But ever since I got involved in animal welfare, which was about 1950, and I know all that is happening in research and how animals are exploited and abused, I-- I just hate the human race.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think of these events very often?

speaker: interviewee
text: I try not to. I mean I have no photos of [? Goldy ?] in my house. Because just looking at every day it would make me terribly sad. But I think it is somehow always with you. But-- but unless something like this comes up, I-- I don't know wallow in it. And we never talk about it. There are quite a few people here who were in the camps and who were my friends, or became my friends. No, we never talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your mother stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother went, of course, what would-- would she do in Czechoslovakia with all that trouble there? She after all still had her Viennese passport. Because she-- she never stopped being an Austrian. So she went back after the war. She actually-- I had to wait before I could run away from Czechoslovakia. I waited till she got the permission to take again, her Persian carpet, everything out of Czechoslovakia. And that was terrible to get the permission for somebody to take things out. And she got the permission to-- to move everything to Vienna. And I think within days, I and my two friends ran away. Because I couldn't leave her a second time, you know. So she-- she-- she did well in Vienna. She married again, and eventually she died. But, I mean, I never went back to Czechoslovakia. I hate them so much. Because I can-- I know I shouldn't hate the whole nation. And, I mean, I married a Czech eventually, and I have Czech friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: But because a Czech guy who-- who-- that gendarme who called [? Goldy, ?] all he had to do is say, don't do it. He could have taken all the food, taken all the money, and-- and sent him back to the ghetto. Why he put him in with the Germans? And I-- I just have such a hatred that I would never, never go back there. What was your reason for telling us your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: The reason is that I feel terribly disappointed with the people who were in the camps. Because I think they didn't learn anything. I mean I shouldn't sit in judgment over people. But the people I know who were in the camps, it didn't make them better human beings. And I find this very disappointing. I thought that if you go through all that misery that it is-- you should-- you should learn from it. And they are just so selfish and self-centered that-- that I live like a recluse actually. Because I have very few people who have my interests, and who-- who understand what-- what it's all about, or what-- why I'm now, against, so tormented with the things that are happening to animals, that-- that-- like I was with [? Goldy. ?] And I-- I would like to do anything as long as I'm alive, I mean, whatever I do to-- to help. My dream is to stop experimentation on-- on animals, mainly on primates. And it just breaks my heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you consider yourself Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm-- I'm just a person. I-- I mean, I'm certainly-- I don't think I'm a Czech. Even if I was born in Prague, I don't think I'm a Czech. I certainly am not a Christian. So I must be Jewish. But-- but if somebody is Orthodox Jewish, they are just as revolting to me than-- than-- than people who are madly Christian. I-- I think that religion is-- is the worst thing in this world. If we could do away with the religion, a lot of those terrible wars would not happen. If we could all have one language, we could all communicate and have no religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think religion-- I don't believe in God. I mean, I-- I did as a child. Because it was done. But when I was in Auschwitz and I remember exactly the moment when-- when I thought now there is just no God. And I never needed God. I think that a lot of people in the camp became very, very religious, and-- and were calling for God to help. And-- and I think it's-- it's very arrogant to ask God to help you. Because what's so special? What would be so special about me that-- that he should help me? If there would be a God, Auschwitz and all those things would have never happened. What was that moment in Auschwitz when you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a moment when we went from the latrine. It must have been in the evening. And-- and those people with the red triangle that were told to us, they were murderers. They were sort of sitting around. And they had those horrible primitive faces. And-- and everything was just so ugly. The whole-- the whole thing, it-- it-- all at once it came to me that there is just no power. And that all this couldn't-- couldn't be, and couldn't happen. And if there is a God, that might as well be me. I think we all are. Every person who is kind and good can do so much to change the world. And there are so few of us around.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you think the events that you went through taught you? What was the main lesson do you think that you learned?

speaker: interviewee
text: The lesson, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if I would have a button to throw up the  human race, I think I would do it. Lesson? I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn from what went through do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I-- I think that coming from this spoiled little brat I was with a nanny, where Mommy was ringing the bell to bring me in, to look at me, and-- and I was dressed like a doll. Of course, the lesson-- the lesson was that I became so much more aware of-- of-- of what life is, and-- and I think what it should be. But the world is so much worse now than I think that when-- when I was a child. Or we didn't know about it. Because there was no TV. And maybe it was always so horrible. But I think the world is just a dreadful, dreadful place. I won't be-- I won't be-- I won't be sad to let it go. But I would still like to-- to achieve something. And I think all that-- that anger I have, is very good. Because that anger keeps me doing things. I-- I don't live to just mollycoddle myself. And I think this is what I learned from the camp that you-- that you're much tougher than you think. And you can cope with so much more. I'm very proud that I went to the camp. I-- I know when I say that once in a while, somebody thinks I'm mad. But I think it-- if wouldn't have gone to the camp, I think I would feel even worse about [? Goldy. ?] At least I-- I did, I tried to share something with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any messages that you want to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Messages? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think that people should be much, much more compassionate to each other, to-- to any-- any living thing. I think like-- like Albert Schweitzer had this saying. I can't think of it now. That we should not look away when there is something horrible. That we-- we should-- we should try to-- to change it. I think that if-- if lot of people would-- would want to make this world a better place, I think it could be done. And that people shouldn't be so self-centered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much for sharing your story with us. It's a a fascinating one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for having me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father. His name is Anton Beran. And I think it was taken on vacation. And I think it would have been about 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Lola Beran. And it was also taken when we were in the summer at some lake in the Sudetenland. And, oh, I think it-- it must be maybe 1930, '33, '35.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father and myself, when we are visiting the non-Jewish grandmother in Vienna. Because I never met my Jewish grandmother. She was dead by the time I was born. It is taken in [INAUDIBLE] in [? Veen. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is taken in-- in the city, in Prague. It's my father and me going for our Saturday stroll. And I think I must have been about-- I don't know-- nine years old at that time. And this is a photo of [? Goldy, ?] Has Selig. And it was taken in Prague about 1940 by a friend of ours. His name is Herbert Loebl. And he lives now in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us about this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken in 1949 when I came to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I-- I'm sorry. I-- I-- well, it is-- it's taken indoors by a friend of mine. But I don't know exactly where. It's a long time ago.
